summary:
The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648 AD) was the most destructive conflict in European history before the World Wars, a catastrophic series of interconnected wars fought primarily in the Holy Roman Empire that killed an estimated 8 million people (through battle, famine, and plague) — reducing the population of some German territories by a third — and ended with the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]], which established the foundations of the modern international state system. The war began as a religious-political conflict within the Holy Roman Empire, triggered by the [[event:2403|Defenestration of Prague]] (1618), when Protestant Bohemian nobles threw Catholic imperial officials from a window and ignited a Bohemian revolt against Habsburg rule. What began as a regional religious conflict rapidly drew in the major powers of Europe: Denmark, Sweden (under [[event:2405|Gustavus Adolphus]]), France, and Spain all intervened, transforming a German religious war into a continental power struggle.

The war passed through four phases (Bohemian, Danish, Swedish, French) with fluctuating fortunes. The Swedish phase was particularly dramatic: Gustavus Adolphus's military innovations (combined arms tactics, mobile field artillery, disciplined infantry) made Sweden briefly the dominant military power in Germany before his death at Lützen (1632). France, though Catholic, intervened on the Protestant side to check Habsburg power — a clear indication that state interest had eclipsed religious solidarity as the primary driver of policy. By the 1630s–1640s, the war had largely dissolved into mutual exhaustion, widespread mercenary warfare, and systematic civilian devastation, immortalized in Hans Heberle's chronicle and the fictional account of Grimmelshausen's Simplicissimus. The Peace of Westphalia's key innovation was the principle of state sovereignty — that rulers were supreme within their territories and no external power (including the papacy) could legitimately intervene in their domestic affairs — establishing the framework of international relations that still formally governs the world.
